network.name: Skynet Nexus News
network.tagline: Real youth stories, worth reading together.
editorialRules: Youth-first: subjects are usually under 25, quoted first.
Family-safe: no violence, war, politics, celebrity drama, or social media pile-ons.
No mainstream news filler: own reporting, primary sources.
Every article includes Kid Take + Family Discussion + glossary for co-reading.

ai.label: AI & Machine Learning
ai.scope: Young ML researchers, student AI projects, model builders, AI for good.

space.label: Space & Aerospace
space.scope: CubeSats, rocketry clubs, NASA challenges, student astronomers and aerospace teams.

robotics.label: Robotics & Automation
robotics.scope: FIRST (FRC/FTC/FLL), VEX, BEST, RoboCup Junior - student teams and matches.

biotech.label: Biotech & Health
biotech.scope: Young bioengineers, health hackers, genetics, med-tech science fair work.

quantum.label: Quantum & Computing
quantum.scope: Quantum computing, semiconductors, computing research by students.

climate.label: Climate & Energy
climate.scope: Young climate innovators, clean energy builds, sustainability projects.

engineering.label: Engineering & Making
engineering.scope: Makers, hardware hackers, civil/mechanical builds, invention conventions.

math.label: Math & Data Science
math.scope: Math olympiads, data science projects, statistics and modeling by youth.

cyber.label: Cybersecurity & Code
cyber.scope: CTF teams, student developers, open-source contributors, security research.

gaming.label: Gaming Tournaments
gaming.scope: Scholastic esports, chess, speedrunning, kid-made games and tournaments.

music.label: Music Festivals
music.scope: YoungArts, All-State, teen songwriters, youth ensembles and festivals.

stem.label: STEM
stem.scope: Legacy: general science, engineering, math and young researchers.

play.label: Play & Design
play.scope: Legacy: Minecraft/Roblox/Scratch creators, chess prodigies, kid-made games.

network.label: Network
network.scope: Editorial notes, launch announcements, network updates.

articles.title: High School Team Wins STEM Prize for Algae-Based Bio-Plastic Packaging
articles.excerpt: A student science team developed an algae-based packaging wrap that degrades in 14 days, providing a green alternative to plastic wrap.
articles.kidTake: Three high school students grew green algae in their classroom and turned it into clear packaging wrap, like plastic wrap. Unlike regular plastic, this AlgaeWrap dissolves and breaks down in the soil in just two weeks, keeping trash out of our oceans.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: Indiana High School Rocketry Club Launches Dual-Stage Rocket to 15,000 Feet
articles.excerpt: The rocketry club "AeroDevs" successfully launched and recovered a dual-stage rocket, capturing high-altitude atmospheric data.
articles.kidTake: A group of high school students built a carbon-fiber rocket that launched nearly three miles into the air! The rocket had two engines that fired one after another. A tiny computer on the rocket recorded scientific data before bringing the rocket back down safely with parachutes.
articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: FIRST Team Builds Assistive Robotics Arm for Libraries
articles.excerpt: FIRST Robotics Team "Circuit Breakers" designed a lightweight, motorized arm to assist library visitors with limited mobility.
articles.kidTake: A high school robotics team built a cool mechanical arm that clips onto a wheelchair. Using a phone joystick, a person can move the arm to reach and pick up books from high library shelves. The students made their designs free so anyone can build one.
articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: High Schooler Wins Quantum Cryptography Logic Game Award
articles.excerpt: Chloe Xu, 17, designed a visual logic game called QubitEncrypt that teaches kids how polarized light secures data from internet eavesdroppers.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old girl named Chloe built an online puzzle game that teaches kids how to send secret messages using physics. In her game, you use colored lasers to encrypt information. If a hacker tries to read the secret code, the lasers change color so you know they are spying.
articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Roblox Adventure Game Teaches Kids Wilderness Survival Skills
articles.excerpt: A team of three high school game designers built "Wild Survival" on Roblox, attracting over 200,000 players with realistic outdoor simulation.
articles.kidTake: Three teenagers built a popular game on Roblox where you learn how to survive in the forest. Players have to build shelters to stay warm, filter water to drink, and find safe wild food. The game teaches real scouting skills while you play with friends.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Teen Violinist Marcus Chen Wins International Paganini Prize in Genoa
articles.excerpt: Marcus Chen, 15, won first place at the international Paganini Competition, performing challenging violin caprices with stunning technique.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old boy named Marcus won one of the hardest violin contests in the world! He played in Italy with a full orchestra and won first place. He even got to play on a famous, 280-year-old violin that belonged to the composer Niccolò Paganini.
articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: Undergrad Math Team Solves Longstanding Grid Congestion Problem
articles.excerpt: A team of three undergraduate mathematicians designed an algorithm that reduces electrical grid overload risks using network graph theory.
articles.kidTake: A team of young mathematicians used math puzzles called graphs to find a better way to send electricity through power lines. Their smart computer math code prevents power lines from getting too hot, which helps stop electricity blackouts during hot summer days.
articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: Cleveland Youth Chess Club Hosts 24-Hour Chess Marathon for Local Charity
articles.excerpt: The Cleveland Youth Chess Club raised over $4,000 by hosting a 24-hour chess marathon, playing hundreds of rapid games for charity.
articles.kidTake: Fifty kids spent a whole day and night playing chess games in a marathon! They streamed their matches online and raised $4,000. They used the money to buy books and fun board games for children recovering in a local hospital.
articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: 14-Year-Old Clara Zhang Builds Gravity-Powered Light for Power Outages
articles.excerpt: Clara Zhang, 14, designed a mechanical light called GravityGlow that uses a system of gears and a slow-falling weight to provide battery-free illumination.
articles.kidTake: A 14-year-old girl named Clara built a lamp that doesn't need batteries or plugs. You just lift a heavy bag of rocks, and as gravity pulls the bag down, it spins gears inside the lamp to make electricity! It gives you free light just from gravity.
articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: High Schooler Devises DNS-Over-HTTPS Tool to Shield School WiFi
articles.excerpt: High school programmer Leo Vance built a lightweight tool that encrypts web address requests, keeping public internet users safe from tracking.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old programmer named Leo built a small, inexpensive device that protects your privacy when you use public WiFi. It encrypts the web addresses you type so that trackers cannot spy on what you are reading or learning online.
articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: Oregon Teenagers Reclaim Bio-Char from Yard Waste to Enrich Soil
articles.excerpt: A team of high school students in Eugene, Oregon, is building backyard kilns to turn garden debris into bio-char, locking carbon away for centuries.
articles.kidTake: A group of teenagers in Oregon built special metal barrels to bake yard waste like branches and leaves without letting them burn. This leaves behind a black charcoal called bio-char. Mixing bio-char into garden soil helps plants grow faster and keeps carbon trapped in the ground so it doesn't warm the Earth.
articles.author: Terra Green

articles.title: Teen Researcher Aaron Patel Invents Wearable Sensor to Monitor Hydration
articles.excerpt: Aaron Patel designed a flexible skin patch that analyzes sweat electrolytes in real-time, sending hydration alerts to a smartphone app.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old runner named Aaron invented a smart skin patch that tells you when you need to drink water. The patch reads your sweat and sends a message to your phone. This helps athletes stay safe and healthy while playing outside in the heat.
articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Tara Sullivan Builds AI to Help Farmers Spot Crop Diseases Early
articles.excerpt: Tara Sullivan, 15, developed a mobile app called LeafScan AI that helps local farmers identify crop diseases in seconds, protecting food supplies.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old girl named Tara built a smart phone app that helps farmers keep their crops healthy. Farmers can take a picture of a leaf, and the app uses artificial intelligence to check if the plant is sick. This helps farmers save their crops and grow more food.
articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: Middle School Science Team Wins Prize for Water Filtration Using Banana Peels
articles.excerpt: The team engineered a simple, low-cost bio-filter that cleans well water using kitchen scraps.
articles.kidTake: Three students built a cheap water filter using dried banana peels and sand. When you pass dirty well water through it, the banana peels absorb heavy metals, cleaning the water so it is safe to drink.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: New Hampshire High School Team Launches High-Altitude Balloon to Capture Cosmic Radiation
articles.excerpt: The "StratoScholars" high school team built a custom sensor payload that successfully tracked radiation levels near the stratosphere.
articles.kidTake: A team of high school students sent a giant helium balloon nearly 18 miles high into the sky! The balloon carried a small capsule with sensors to measure radiation from outer space. The capsule parachuted back down, and the kids found it in the forest using GPS.
articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: High School Club Designs Autonomous Beach-Cleaning Rover for Coastal Cleanup
articles.excerpt: The "EcoBots" club engineered a motorized rover that clears small plastic debris from community beaches.
articles.kidTake: A high school robotics team built a motorized sand-sifting robot that cleans beaches. The robot drives along the shore, scoops up sand, shakes it through a metal screen, and collects tiny plastic trash pieces while leaving the clean sand behind.
articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: 16-Year-Old Programmer Builds Visual Game Explaining Quantum Entanglement
articles.excerpt: Aris Thorne built a browser game where players must entangle particles to solve logic puzzles.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old programmer built an online maze game called SpookyAction. In the game, you move two magic particles that are linked together. When you move one particle, the other one moves the exact same way, helping you solve puzzles.
articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Teenager Designs Modular Treehouse Playground for Children with Physical Disabilities
articles.excerpt: Amara Okafor built a modular model for an inclusive playground with ramps, sensory panels, and swings.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old girl named Amara designed a cool treehouse playground that kids in wheelchairs can play in. The design uses wooden ramps winding around trees, tactile paths, and inclusive swings so all kids can play together.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Youth Orchestra Records Album Composed Entirely by High School Students
articles.excerpt: The 80-member youth symphony recorded a professional album featuring original teen compositions.
articles.kidTake: A youth orchestra made of 80 kids recorded a music album. All seven symphonic songs on the album were written by high school students. The kids stood next to the conductor to help guide the recording.
articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: 17-Year-Old Data Scientist Wins National Modeling Prize for Traffic Optimization Algorithm
articles.excerpt: Sophia Patel designed a coordinate-free queue model that reduces morning traffic delays by 25 percent.
articles.kidTake: An 17-year-old girl named Sophia wrote a math paper that explains how to stop school traffic jams. She used math models to rearrange drop-off lanes and traffic lights, cutting morning traffic delays by 25 percent.
articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Wins National High School Chess and Speedrunning Double-Title
articles.excerpt: Kai Jenkins captured first place in the national scholastic rapid chess tournament and set a new retro game record.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old kid named Kai won two national gaming titles on the same weekend! He won a big rapid chess tournament and set a world speedrunning record for a retro arcade game by beating it in 11 minutes.
articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: Students Build Prototype Solar-Powered Water Distiller for Coastal Communities
articles.excerpt: A three-student team engineered a solar distiller that yields two gallons of clean drinking water daily from sea water.
articles.kidTake: Three students built a sun-powered machine that turns salty ocean water into clean drinking water. The machine uses curved mirrors to heat up the water until it turns into steam, leaving the salt behind, and then cools the steam into fresh water.
articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: Student Programmer Builds Chrome Extension to Shield Kids from Online Ad Trackers
articles.excerpt: Leo Pixel built "PrivacyPup", a privacy extension that intercepts tracking scripts on popular school websites.
articles.kidTake: A 14-year-old kid named Leo built a privacy extension for school computers. It features a cartoon dog that bark-blocks tracking scripts, keeping companies from spying on what you learn online.
articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: 16-Year-Old Student Isolates Soil Bacteria That Accelerates Composting
articles.excerpt: Chloe Kim discovered a local soil bacterium that breaks down organic starch and cellulose in half the usual time.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old girl named Chloe found a special helper bacteria in forest soil. When you add this bacteria to kitchen waste like apple cores and potato peels, it helps them rot and turn into healthy garden soil twice as fast.
articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Coder Trains AI Model to Translate Sign Language in Real Time
articles.excerpt: Liam Patel built a lightweight AI translator that translates American Sign Language (ASL) finger-spelling instantly.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old kid named Liam built a computer program that listens to hand signs instead of voices. If a person makes sign language gestures in front of a webcam, the AI instantly speaks the letters out loud in English.
articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: Watching the Nexus Grow: Our First Week of Community and Connection
articles.excerpt: Editor-in-Chief Jeffrey Hunt reports on our expanding family, new features, and the road ahead.
articles.kidTake: It has been one week since Skynet Nexus News started! Lots of families are reading our daily stories together. We updated our AI reporter names and built a new dashboard. Every Sunday, our editor Jeffrey Hunt will write a new update in this thread to share our news with you.
articles.author: Jeffrey Hunt

articles.title: The Architecture of Curiosity: How Youth STEM Shaped America
articles.excerpt: A special edition tracing how young inventors, flight pioneers, and computing trailblazers built the foundations of American science and engineering.
articles.kidTake: For 250 years, young inventors and students in America have built amazing new technologies. From Benjamin Franklin's early experiments to the Wright brothers' first airplane and modern student robotics contests, young people have always driven progress. This special edition explores five big milestones in STEM history led by curious young minds.
articles.author: Jeffrey Hunt

articles.title: 17-Year-Old Researcher Iris Shen Discovers Plastic-Eating Bacteria in Local Soil
articles.excerpt: 17-year-old high school student Iris Shen isolated a strain of bacteria from a local landfill that can break down polyethylene plastic, offering a green solution to waste.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old student named Iris discovered a special type of bacteria in landfill soil that can eat plastic! The bacteria use special proteins to break down tough plastic bags and bottles into harmless natural materials, helping clean up the environment.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: High School CubeSat Project 'SkyBolt-1' Launched into Orbit by NASA Rocket
articles.excerpt: Built by a team of high school students in Seattle, the miniature satellite 'SkyBolt-1' was launched into orbit from Cape Canaveral to study cosmic rays.
articles.kidTake: A group of high school students built their own miniature satellite shaped like a small cube. It was launched into space on a rocket! Now, the satellite is orbiting high above Earth and sending back scientific data to their classroom radio.
articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: Student Robotics Team 'Flying Hedgehogs' Builds Autonomous River Cleaning Robot
articles.excerpt: High school robotics team 2898 designed an autonomous floating robot that utilizes lidar and trash claw mechanisms to clean up plastic waste in local rivers.
articles.kidTake: A high school robotics team built a floating robot that cleans up rivers. The robot uses laser eyes called lidar to steer around obstacles like logs while a conveyor belt scoops up plastic bottles and trash from the water.
articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: Undergraduate Student Samira Patel Builds Quantum Computing Simulator for Kids
articles.excerpt: Samira Patel, 19, created QubitPlay, a visual simulator that helps young students understand complex concepts like quantum superposition and qubits.
articles.kidTake: A 19-year-old student named Samira built a fun computer game called QubitPlay. The game helps kids understand how super-fast quantum computers work. It uses spinning globes and space puzzles to explain how computers can think in new ways.
articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Kid Game Design Team Wins Roblox Innovation Award for Adventure Game 'EcoQuest'
articles.excerpt: Created by three 12-year-old friends, the Roblox game 'EcoQuest' has received a Roblox Innovation Award for its educational gameplay and creative design.
articles.kidTake: Three 12-year-old friends built their own game inside Roblox called EcoQuest. In the game, you swim underwater to clean up trash and save sea animals. Their game won a big design award and has been played by over 100,000 kids!
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Teen Cellist Bella Correia Performs as Soloist with London Symphony Youth Orchestra
articles.excerpt: 16-year-old cellist Bella Correia delivered a breathtaking performance of Edward Elgar's Cello Concerto as the featured soloist at Royal Albert Hall.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old girl named Bella played a giant string instrument called a cello at a famous concert hall in London. She was the star soloist, playing a very hard piece of music with a whole orchestra of teenagers backing her up. The audience loved her performance and gave her a giant round of applause!
articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: U.S. Math Team Wins Gold Medal at International Mathematical Olympiad in Oslo
articles.excerpt: Six high school students representing the United States won first place at the 2026 International Mathematical Olympiad in Oslo, Norway, earning four gold medals.
articles.kidTake: Six high school students went to Norway to compete in a giant math contest against kids from 110 countries. The U.S. team won first place! The math problems were very hard puzzles, and the students had to write long explanations to prove their answers were correct.
articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: 14-Year-Old Chess Prodigy Kai Zhou Lan Climbs Rankings at FIDE Youth Championship
articles.excerpt: 14-year-old chess prodigy Kai Zhou Lan achieved a standout performance at the FIDE World Cadet Cup, earning 140 international rating points and climbing the world rankings.
articles.kidTake: A 14-year-old boy named Kai played in a big chess tournament against kids from all over the world. He played so well that he won fifth place in the world for his age group! Kai says the secret to winning is staying calm and thinking carefully about your moves before you touch the pieces.
articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Lily Ortiz Designs Low-Cost Solar Distiller for Clean Water
articles.excerpt: Using recycled acrylic sheets and black paint, 15-year-old Lily Ortiz engineered a low-cost solar water distiller that purifies dirty water using simple evaporation.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old girl named Lily built a special box that cleans dirty water using heat from the sun. The sun heats the water until it turns into invisible steam, leaving the dirt behind. The steam then cools down and turns back into clean water that is safe to drink.
articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: High Schooler Leo Vance Builds Open-Source Tool to Help Families Block Tracking
articles.excerpt: Concerned about how companies track kids online, 18-year-old Leo Vance built GuardDog, a free browser extension that blocks tracking scripts and advertising beacons.
articles.kidTake: An 18-year-old named Leo built a free computer tool called GuardDog that protects your family when you go online. It acts like a digital shield, blocking invisible trackers that try to watch what websites you visit. This keeps your personal information safe and private.
articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: Nairobi Teenagers Build Solar-Powered Battery Chargers from Recycled E-Waste
articles.excerpt: High school students in Nairobi, Kenya, are combatting electronic waste by converting old laptop batteries and broken solar panels into portable chargers.
articles.kidTake: A team of creative teenagers in Kenya collected old, broken laptop batteries and solar panels that people threw away. They fixed the good parts inside and built solar-powered phone chargers! Now, families in their neighborhood can charge their phones for free using energy from the sun.
articles.author: Terra Green

articles.title: Texas Teen Sophie Chen Creates ML Tool to Predict Organ Transplant Rejection
articles.excerpt: Sophie Chen, 17, designed an AI model called OrganShield that predicts heart transplant rejection rates using blood samples, replacing painful tissue biopsies.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old girl named Sophie made a computer program that helps patients who get new hearts. Instead of doctors doing painful tests, Sophie's program reads a simple blood test to make sure the patient's body is accepting the new heart. This makes healing much easier and less painful.
articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: 16-Year-Old Samay Kumar Develops AI to Track Wildfire Smoke from Space
articles.excerpt: Using satellite images from NOAA, 16-year-old Samay Kumar created a machine learning model that detects wildfire smoke plumes hours faster than human teams.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old student named Samay built a smart computer program that looks at photos of Earth from space. The program uses artificial intelligence to spot wildfire smoke very quickly. This helps firefighters know where fires are starting so they can put them out before they get too big.
articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: Ziyu Shao, 16, Just Won the 50-Year-Old Bachauer Piano Competition in Salt Lake City
articles.excerpt: The five finalists were all 16 or 17 years old. They played Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, and Tchaikovsky with a full symphony behind them. And they're calling themselves "the prodigies of tomorrow."
articles.kidTake: In Salt Lake City, Utah, five kids around 16 or 17 years old played the piano in front of a whole big orchestra — the Utah Symphony — in a very famous concert hall. It was the final of a competition that started with 316 kids from 28 different countries. A 16-year-old girl named Ziyu Shao, from China, won first place. All five kids stayed with families in Salt Lake City who let them practice on their pianos every day for two weeks!
articles.author: Riley Chen

articles.title: MIT Students Just Designed a Moon Base — and NASA Handed Them Five Trophies
articles.excerpt: 35 students. Eight departments. One shared blueprint for humanity's first permanent home on the Moon — and NASA judges couldn't stop giving them awards.
articles.kidTake: A bunch of college students at MIT spent a whole school year designing what a real, permanent Moon base could look like — power lines, cell towers, and even little robots that turn Moon dirt into bricks. NASA had a contest for who could design it best, and the MIT students won five awards, including first place. Their ideas might really get used when astronauts start living on the Moon in a few years.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: Eight U.S. Kids Cracked the Top 10 at the FIDE World Cadet Chess Cup
articles.excerpt: No U.S. medals this year — but eight of the 23 young Americans on the delegation finished in the top 10 of their section, and one 10-year-old picked up 140 international rating points on the way.
articles.kidTake: In a country called Georgia (not the U.S. state — the country next to Turkey!), 662 kids age 12 and younger from all over the world played a giant chess tournament that lasted two weeks. Eight kids from Team USA finished in the top 10 of their age group. A 10-year-old boy named Kai Zhou Lan almost won a medal — he started ranked 40th and ended up fifth in the world. And a 7-year-old named Varun Karthik Turaga finished fourth in the whole world in his age group.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Beaverton Robotics Team Went Viral Chasing Jordan's World Cup Squad. It Worked.
articles.excerpt: About 25 high schoolers from four Beaverton, Oregon schools spent June building not just robots, but a viral internet campaign — all to bring FIRST Robotics to a country on the other side of the world.
articles.kidTake: A group of about 25 high schoolers in Oregon build big competition robots. Their favorite World Cup team, from Jordan, came to practice in Portland — so the students made a new short video every day for three weeks asking the players to come visit their robot shop. The videos went so viral that a huge news station in Jordan showed them on TV. The soccer players haven't come by yet, but the students say the real goal was to get more kids in Jordan excited about building robots.
articles.author: Maya Ortiz

articles.title: Welcome to Skynet Nexus - The Signal Goes Live
articles.excerpt: A new daily news network for families who want to learn together - real youth achievements in science, engineering, robotics, creative play, and music. Free forever. First full edition drops tomorrow.
articles.kidTake: This is a new news website! Every morning it gives you four short stories about cool things kids around the world are doing - in science, robots, creative games, and music.
articles.author: The Newsroom
